title: 1. Liquor Registration – Draft for Consultation
text:
BUSINESS LICENCE GUIDANCE ON LIQUOR
ESTABLISHMENTS
Introduction
This Guide is intended to provide informa3on about the requirements for Liquor Establishments within
the context of business licencing. It should be read in conjunc3on with the Business Licence Act, 2023
(“BL Act”), the Business Licence (Amendment) Act, 2025 (“BL Amendment Act”) and the Business
Licence Regula3ons, 2023 (“BL Regula3ons”), which can be found on the website of the Department of
Inland Revenue (“DIR”). If there is a discrepancy between this Guide and the BL Act, BL Amendment Act
and the BL, the law will prevail.
Current changes
The BL (Amendment) Act has introduced a pre-cer3fica3on process for all liquor establishments that
must be completed prior to the grant of an annual Business Licence.
The new requirements for Liquor Establishments are set out in this Guide.
What is a Liquor Establishment?
For the purposes of the BL Act, the term ‘liquor establishment’ applies generally to any business that
sells, exposes or offers liquor for sale.
Liquor is defined in the BL Act as meaning “every descrip3on of alcoholic spirits, wines, ale, beer, porter,
stout, cider, perry and other malt liquor, and any fermented or dis3lled liquor”
Liquor establishments therefore include but are not limited to the following:
• Liquor Stores
• Bars
• Restaurants that serve alcohol
• Hotels that serve alcohol
• Members clubs that serve alcohol
• Nightclubs that serve alcohol
Obtaining or renewing an annual business licence – Registration Process
A business licence is required to operate any liquor establishment in The Bahamas.
In order to obtain or renew an annual business licence, applicants must complete a registra3on process
by submiYng the prescribed applica3on form, Registra+on Applica+on Form together with the
suppor3ng documents, and then obtain a Cer3ficate of Registra3on.
1

Upon receipt of an applica3on for a Cer3ficate of Registra3on and payment of the applicable fee, the DIR
shall make a thorough inves3ga3on to determine whether the applicant and the premises out of which
the liquor business will operate, qualify for a license. The registra3on process aims to protect public
safety and uphold moral standards and to prevent unregulated and unsuitable methods of distribu3on.
An applica3on for a Cer3ficate of Registra3on will be denied if either the applicant or the premises for
which a license has been applied, do not qualify for a license.
REGISTRATION PROCESS
Phase 1 – Applica+on for registra+on as a liquor establishment
A. Time to apply
New businesses are required to submit a registra3on applica3on at least 90 days before applying
for the annual business licence.
Applicants for renewals of annual business licences for exis3ng businesses must submit
their registra3on renewal applica3on by 31st December each year.
B. Requirements to be met before applying
Applicants must ensure that they have the following approvals before beginning the registra3on
process
1. An approved trading name. The applica3on for an approved trading name can be found at
on the DIR website at [XXXXX].
2. Necessary agency approvals from the agencies listed below. Approval from agencies must be
dated and/or issued no later than 3 months prior to the date of applica3on for the
Cer3ficate of Registra3on.
C. Applica+ons linked to specific premises
Each Cer3ficate of Registra3on is acached to a specific liquor establishment at a par3cular
loca3on (‘the registered premises’). An applicant must therefore submit a separate applica3on in
rela3on to each of the registered premises where the applicant operates or intends to carry on a
liquor business.
D. Registra+on Fee
2

Registra3on Fees must be paid in order to complete your applica3on for a Cer3ficate of
Registra3on and are as set forth in the Table below. Fees are payable with respect each of the
registered premises being cer3fied.
Ader submiYng your registra3on applica3on, you will receive an invoice sta3ng the total
amount of fees to be paid. Payment should be made through the Portal at [XXXXXXX]
Registra3on
Type of Establishment
Fee
Liquor Store $100.00
Bar, Restaurant or
$50.00
other
E. Procedure for applying
Applica3ons for registra3on or the renewal of a registra3on should be in the prescribed form,
Registra3on Applica3on Form, which can be found on the DIR website at hcps://
inlandrevenue.finance.gov.bs
Forms for renewals of an exis3ng Cer3ficate of Registra3on or first 3me applica3ons for a Cer3ficate
of Registra3on must be accompanied by the following documents, where applicable-
• Lease or rental agreement for the business loca3on or Proof of ownership eg. Copy of
conveyance.
• Cer3ficate of Incorpora3on
• Beneficial ownership declara3on
• Personal or Business NIB Informa3on (for applicant)
• Approved trading name
• Required regulatory and departmental approvals
o Department of Environmental Health Services (Sanita3on/Waste Removal)
o Cer3fica3on from Bahamas Agricultural Health & Food Safety (BAHFSA)
o Ministry of Works/Building Control (Inspec3on)
o RBPF (Fire Inspec3on Report)
o Inspec3on Report from Royal Bahamas Police Force/Business Inspec3on and Licencing
Sec3on (Criminal ac3vity, criminal background)
o Department of Physical Planning (Zoning, Parking)
Persons who have an exis3ng Business Licence but are applying for Cer3ficate of Registra3on for the first
3me must re-submit the above documents.
Phase 2 – Public Consulta+on
A. New registra+ons:,
3

Prior to approving a new applica3on for registra3on, the DIR will publish a No3ce of Public
Consulta3on invi3ng comments from the general public and interested par3es. The no3ce will specify
the date, loca3on and manner in which the public consulta3ve process will take place. The no3ce will
also request wricen comments which must be submiced using the form provided on the DIR website at
[XXXXXX]
The no3ce may be posted via:
o Local newspaper
o DIR website
o Signage at the business loca3on
B. Renewal of registra+on:
Before the renewal of registra3on, the DIR will invite public comments for considera3on via its website
or other approved channels. Public comments must be submiced in the form provided on the DIR
website at [XXXXXXXX]
Phase 3 – Factors taken into account in determining approval of registra+on
In making a decision to register an establishment, any of the following factors may be taken into account:
• Criminal background check of the applicant, officers or directors of the applicant
• Suitability of the premises – The sale of liquor requires premises that meet specific
standards to ensure a safe, controlled, and responsible environment for both customers
and the surrounding community. The suitability depends on the classifica3on of the
liquor establishment and the methods of distribu3on.
On-premise consump+on - See Specific Requirements
o Compliance with fire, safety, health and building regula3ons
o Size and capacity – Must be appropriate based on use of the premises
o Physical layout and design –
▪ Must support responsible alcohol service and customer safety
▪ Clearly defined areas for dining, bar service, sea3ng areas, bathrooms,
kitchen and storage areas.
▪ Adequate restroom facili3es based on occupancy
▪ Maximum occupancy limit
Off-premise consump+on – See Specific Requirements
o Compliance with fire, safety, health and building regula3ons
o Size and capacity – Must be appropriate based on use of premises and volume
of liquor sales/stored
o Physical layout and design –
o Clear entry/exit points
o Avoid obstructed sight lines of all areas where alcohol is served.
Drive-Through Sales
4

No liquor establishment will be cer3fied which sells, dispenses, or delivers
alcoholic beverages to customers through a drive-through service, including but
not limited to:
▪ Any service that allows a customer to remain in a motor vehicle while
comple3ng the transac3on.
▪ Any window or portal designed or used to serve customers in vehicles.
Window Sales
The sale of alcohol through any exterior-facing window, sliding hatch, or other
architectural opening that allows alcohol to be handed to a customer located
outside the registered premises is strictly prohibited. No Take A-way (window)
Liquor sales will be permiced.
Cages or Bars
The sale of liquor must not be conducted through any cage, bar or physical
barrier that physically separates staff from patrons and prevents direct, open
interac3on during liquor sales. Cages, bars or physical barriers are considered
any structure resembling a security cage, pass-through window or reinforced
par33on that restricts visibility, communica3on or accessibility.
Cages, bars and physical barriers signal that the establishment is in high-risk
loca3on not suitable for public liquor sales.
A limited excep3on may be granted where
• A legi3mate safety concern has been demonstrated due to the area’s
elevated risk level, however it is determined that the loca3on is not so
high-risk as to render it unsuitable for liquor sales and
• Alterna3ve security measures have been deemed insufficient and
• The applicant has met all other requirements for the type of licence
applied for.
Separate Entrances
To ensure controlled access all liquor retail establishments shall be required to
have clearly designated and physically separate entrances and exits from those
of any adjoining or neighboring businesses.
The entrances and exits must be clearly marked, accessible, and designed to
promote the safe and orderly flow of customers exclusively to and from the
liquor sales area. Shared access points are strictly prohibited to uphold these
standards.
Example, no other business can operate from the premises registered to sell
liquor. If there is an adjoining business, that business cannot share the same
entry and exit as the liquor establishment.
• Proximity to schools or places of worship
5

o No Liquor Establishment should be cer3fied unless it is 700 feet away from
places of worship, schools, residences and other liquor establishments. This
requirement does not apply to full-service restaurants.
o Applicants with pre-exis3ng Liquor Establishments may, however, by formal
wricen request at the 3me of submiYng their applica3on, show compelling and
sa3sfactory reasons as to why a Cer3ficate of Registra3on should be granted
notwithstanding non-compliance with the distance requirement. Such
applica3ons will only be approved in excep3onal circumstances.
o Specifically, such applicants must provide clear and documented evidence
demonstra3ng that they sa3sfy all of the following criteria -
▪ The liquor establishment has been in con3nuous opera3on for more
than 10 years prior to the applica3on date or it was established before
any school, place of worship or residen3al community in the designated
area came into existence.
▪ The liquor establishment has a proven track record of compliance with
all relevant laws and regula3ons.
▪ No formal complaints regarding public disturbances have been filed
against the establishment by community groups, including schools,
place of worship, or local residents, within the past 3 years eg. Loitering,
noise, public alterca3ons.
▪ The establishment has obtained lecers of support from authorized
representa3ves of affected schools, places of worship, community
leaders within the designated area.
▪ The establishment has adequate security and safety measures in place
to ensure that minors cannot access alcohol eg. Surveillance systems,
trained staff, ID checks.
▪ The establishment is opera3ng within a legally designated commercial
zone.
▪ The establishment operates during different hours than school or place
of worship events and there is no evidence of interference with the
educa3onal or religious ac3vi3es nearby.
▪ Signage is discreet and establishment does not display adver3sements
that target minors or are visible from schools or places of worship.
• Community satura3on (i.e. whether the area is already adequately serviced).
A community will be considered to be ‘adequately serviced’ where the exis3ng number
and distribu3on of liquor establishments in a designated area already sufficiently meet
the needs and demands of the community and ader considera3on of the factors below
it has been determined that no new establishment is necessary or jus3fiable.
o Popula3on size and density
o Number and proximity of exis3ng licensed premises;
▪ Number and type of liquor establishments in a geographic area
▪ Number and type of liquor establishments per square mile/km
o Number of establishments compared to the size of the popula3on
6

▪ Ra3o of liquor establishments to popula3on in a geographic area
o Consump3on pacerns;
o Community concerns or objec3ons;
o Impact on public health, safety, and order.
• Past tax and regula3on compliance history
o Applicants must provide evidence of compliance with tax legisla3on and other
regulatory requirements.
• Any other reasonable ground
o Liquor Establishment located in high crime district.
o History of noise complaints or police involvement
If approval is granted, a Cer3ficate of Registra3on will be issued. The Cer3ficate of Registra3on does not
grant any authority for persons to operate a liquor establishment. Obtaining a Cer3ficate of Registra3on
is merely a mandatory requirement before applying for or renewing an annual business licence.
The Cer3ficate of Registra3on must be conspicuously displayed at the registered premises to which it
relates and must be produced upon request by officials.
Phase 4 – Applica+on for Business Licence
Ader an applicant obtains a Cer3ficate of Registra3on, the applicant must s3ll apply for and be granted
an annual business licence in order to operate a liquor establishment.
Applicants are required to complete and submit their applica3on for a Business Licence through
www.vat.revenue.gov.bs.
Applicants are only required to upload the Cer3ficate of Registra3on. Applicants will not be required to
upload any forms previously submiced during the registra3on process.
Types of annual business licences granted
Business licences may be granted in the following categories –
o Liquor Stores – sale by wholesale and retail of liquor for off-premise consump3on. No
consump3on permiced onsite.
o Wholesale Liquor – sale by wholesale of liquor in sealed containers for off-premise consump3on.
No consump3on permiced onsite.
o Breweries, Wineries and Dis3lleries – sale by wholesale and retail of liquor for on-premise or off-
premise consump3on.
o Bar or Lounge – sale by retail of liquor for on-premise consump3on. No food service.
o Proprietary or Members Clubs – sale by retail to club members and their guests for on-premise
consump3on. Premises include Club grounds.
o Nightclub – sale by retail of liquor for on-premise consump3on
o Restaurant and Bar – sale by retail of liquor for on-premise and only if served with food – casual
dining
7

o Full Service Restaurant - sale by retail of liquor for on-premise and only if served with food
A Proprietary Club or Members Club must comply with the following
a. The rules of the club shall be in wri3ng and shall
i. provide for an elected management commicee or governing body
ii. regulate who can be a member and provide a genuine selec3on process for members.
iii. Provide that no guest can be supplied with liquor unless on the invita3on of and in the
company of a member
b. The Club must require a subscrip3on fee payable by members.
c. The Club must have no less than 25 members.
d. The Club must not be open to members of the public simply by paying an entry fee.
e. The Club must not be a trade organiza3on.
Party buses (if liquor is sold or exposed or offered for sale)
• The vehicle must be cer3fied for a Bar Licence or Restaurant and Bar Licence.
• No service of alcohol is allowed while vehicle is in mo3on.
Mobile Bars
• Only mobile bars with a permanent place of business can be cer3fied.
• Must meet the requirements to obtain a Bar Licence or Restaurant and Bar Licence.
When is a Certificate of Registration not required?
• Complimentary Alcohol – Where liquor is offered for free without any direct or indirect charge,
no cer3fica3on is required. If, however, the business intends to receive money, goods or other
benefits in exchange for the liquor, whether directly or indirectly, cer3fica3on is required. Eg.
Where an entry fee or bundled service includes free drinks as part of a package, cer3fica3on is
required.
• Aircrad operators – No Cer3ficate is required to sell alcohol on board the aircrad while the
aircrad is in The Bahamas
• Cruise ships – No cer3fica3on is required to sell liquor on board any ship calling at The Bahamas
• Gid baskets and flowers – No Cer3fica3on is required for a business that sells gid baskets or
bouquets of flowers which are packaged for sale as such and includes no more than 2 litres of
liquor in each gid.
8

• The Cer3ficate of Registra3on is not a requirement for liquor establishments located in Freeport,
Grand Bahama. Liquor establishments on all other private islands are required to comply with
the cer3fica3on process.
Types of establishment that will not be granted a Certificate of Registration
• Take Away Restaurants
• Liquor sold through walk-up windows
• Liquor sold through any cage, bar or physical barrier
• Drive-through Liquor Stores
• Supermarkets and gas sta3ons
Enforcement and Compliance
The DIR is empowered under the BL Act to monitor, inves3gate, and enforce compliance with all business
licensing requirements. For liquor establishments, this includes both the Business Licence and the
required Cer3ficate of Registra3on.
The Secretary may suspend, amend, cancel, or impose restric3ons on a liquor establishment's Business
Licence and/or Cer3ficate of Registra3on if:
• The applicant/licensee is not compliant with tax laws, including VAT, Real Property Tax, Business
Licence Tax, or Na3onal Insurance contribu3ons
• The applicant/licensee breaches a condi3on of the licence or provides false or misleading
informa3on in their applica3on
• The applicant/licensee fails to meet or maintain the requirements for registra3on as a liquor
establishment (for example: unsuitable premises, community objec3ons, criminal history)
• The liquor establishment contravenes public health, safety, or zoning regula3ons
• The business is opera3ng in a disorderly or unsafe manner, including unsanitary condi3ons, fire
hazards, or inadequate security or supervision
• The applicant/licencee has breached a payment arrangement or failed to meet obliga3ons under
a tax ruling or compliance agreement
The BL Act also provides for certain offences in rela3on to the sale of liquor, for example, selling liquor
without a business licence, selling liquor to a person under the age of eighteen years.
Objections and Appeals
The BL Act provides for a person to formally object to a decision by the Secretary not to grant a licence
and for an appeal to the Tax Appeal Commission against a decision by Secretary made ader considering
the objec3on.
9

Frequently Asked Questions
1. If I have several outlets, do I a need a separate Cer+fica+on of Registra+on for each outlet?
▪ Yes, you must obtain a separate Cer3ficate of Registra3on for each outlet.
2. If I change the loca+on of my liquor establishment can my Cer+ficate of Registra+on be
transferred or do I have to apply for a new Cer+ficate?
▪ Each Cer3ficate of Registra3on is in rela3on to a specific loca3on. Addi3onal documents
rela3ng to the new loca3on will have to be submiced.
3. AZer submi[ng my applica+on for a Cer+ficate of Registra+on, how long will the applica+on
take to be processed?
▪ An applica3on for a new Cer3ficate of Registra3on may take up to six weeks** to process
ader a completed applica3on is submiced.
4. AZer receiving a Cer+ficate of Registra+on and applying for a Business License, how long will the
applica+on for the Business Licence take to be processed?
▪ Once the applica3on is submiced and the Cer3ficate of Registra3on is uploaded, the
applica3on for a Business Licence should take 2 working days to process.
5. If I have an exis+ng annual Business Licence, will I automa+cally be granted a Cer+ficate of
Registra+on?
▪ A new Cer3ficate of Registra3on is not dependant on the existence of a business licence but
will be determined based on whether the applica3on meets the statutory requirements,
6. Can I operate a Liquor Establishment from my home?
▪ No, Liquor establishments cannot be operated from proper3es that are located in areas
zoned for residen3al purposes by the Department of Physical Planning.
10
